patterns.title: Bottom microphone thermal sensor failure (mic1)
patterns.diagnosis: The mic1 NTC thermal sensor on the lightning/charging flex cable is disconnected or damaged.

probabilities.description: The NTC sensor is soldered on the flex. Flex replacement resolves.
Codec IC (338S00509) not reading sensor. Requires micro-soldering.
Standalone NTC failure. Replaceable with micro-soldering.

repair_steps: 2. Test bottom microphone in Voice Memos (record + playback)
3. Verify thermal sensor reads in diagnostics
4. If persists: inspect codec IC under microscope
test_procedure: Voice Memos: record 10s, play back — should hear clear audio from bottom mic
Phone call: speaker on mute, other party should hear you clearly
Diagnostics: Settings > Privacy > Analytics > check for new panic-full logs

patterns.title: Side/rear microphone thermal sensor failure (mic2)
patterns.diagnosis: The mic2 NTC thermal sensor (near volume buttons or rear mic) is disconnected.

probabilities.description: mic2 sensor on the rear camera or side flex. Replace flex.
Connector dislodged during previous repair.
Trace to mic2 sensor broken. Requires micro-soldering.

repair_steps: 1. Reseat or replace rear camera/mic flex cable
2. Check FPC connector on logic board under microscope
3. Test all microphones (Voice Memos, speakerphone, Siri)
test_procedure: Voice Memos: record with phone facing away (rear mic test)
Monitor Analytics for new panic-full logs after repair

patterns.title: Top microphone thermal sensor failure (mic3)
patterns.diagnosis: The mic3 NTC thermal sensor near the earpiece/top speaker is disconnected.

probabilities.description: mic3 is on the earpiece/front sensor flex. Replace flex.
Flex damaged during screen replacement. Reseat or replace.
FPC connector damaged. Micro-soldering required.

repair_steps: 2. Verify all front sensors reconnected properly
4. If persists: inspect FPC connector on board
test_procedure: Siri activation and voice recognition test
Check for new panic logs after 24h

patterns.title: Ambient light sensor failure (ALS)
patterns.diagnosis: The ambient light sensor on the front flex cable is disconnected or damaged. Auto-brightness will not work.

probabilities.description: ALS is on the proximity/earpiece flex. Replace flex.
Flex not transferred or damaged during screen swap.
ALS controller IC failed. Micro-soldering required.

repair_steps: 2. If aftermarket screen: ensure ALS flex is transferred from original
4. If persists: ALS IC on board (micro-soldering)
test_procedure: Auto-brightness: cover sensor, screen should dim

patterns.title: Proximity sensor failure
patterns.diagnosis: The proximity sensor is disconnected. Screen stays on during calls (burns ear, pocket dials).

probabilities.description: Proximity sensor on the earpiece/front flex. Replace flex.
Aftermarket screen without proximity flex. Transfer from OEM.
FPC connector pins bent or corroded.

repair_steps: 1. Replace or reseat proximity/earpiece flex cable
2. For aftermarket screens: transfer OEM proximity flex
3. Test proximity during phone call (screen should turn off near ear)
4. If persists: inspect board FPC connector
test_procedure: Phone call: hold phone to ear, screen should turn off
Cover proximity sensor with finger — screen should blank
Check Settings > Phone > Proximity Sensor behavior

patterns.title: Barometer/pressure sensor failure (PRS0)
patterns.diagnosis: The PRS0 barometric pressure sensor is disconnected. Located on the charging port flex cable.

probabilities.description: PRS0 is on the lightning flex. Replace the entire flex.
Barometer IC failed. Usually resolves with flex replacement.
Trace to barometer broken on board.

repair_steps: 1. Replace lightning/charging port flex cable assembly
2. Test barometer: Compass app > elevation reading
test_procedure: Compass app: check if elevation/altitude is displayed
Weather app: verify local pressure reading
Health app: check flights climbed tracking

patterns.title: Battery temperature sensor failure (TG0B)
patterns.diagnosis: The battery NTC temperature sensor is disconnected or damaged. Device may refuse to charge.

probabilities.description: Battery flex not properly connected. Reseat connector.
NTC on battery flex is damaged. Replace battery.
Battery FPC connector pins damaged.

repair_steps: 1. Disconnect and reseat battery connector firmly
3. Inspect board battery connector under microscope
test_procedure: Check battery health: Settings > Battery > Battery Health
Verify charging works (both wired and wireless if supported)
Monitor temperature during use (should not overheat)

patterns.title: Battery voltage/fuel gauge sensor failure (TG0V)
patterns.diagnosis: The battery fuel gauge IC (bq27541) cannot communicate. Battery percentage may be inaccurate.

probabilities.description: Battery flex not seated. Reseat connector.
bq27541 chip on battery failed. Replace battery.
Communication line to fuel gauge broken on board.

repair_steps: 2. Replace battery if reseating doesn't help
3. Check gas gauge communication lines on board (advanced)
test_procedure: Check battery percentage accuracy (charge to 100%, use, verify drain is linear)
Verify no unexpected shutdowns at >20% battery

patterns.title: Thermal monitoring service failure
patterns.diagnosis: thermalmonitord crashed or hung, usually due to a missing/disconnected thermal sensor.

probabilities.description: A sensor cable is damaged or not seated. Check panic string for 'Missing sensor' to identify which.
PMU or thermal controller IC fault.
Rare. Check if specific to one iOS version.

repair_steps: 1. Identify which sensor is missing from panic string
2. Map sensor to physical component (mic1=lightning flex, als=proximity flex, etc.)
4. If no sensor identified: check all flex connections, reseat
test_procedure: Monitor for recurrence over 24h after repair
Check Analytics for new crash logs

patterns.title: GPU firmware lockup / hang
patterns.diagnosis: The GPU core locked up, triggering a firmware reset or kernel panic.

probabilities.description: Cold solder joint under SoC. Common after drop or thermal cycling.
GPU thermal paste dried, heatsink not seated.
Check if specific to one iOS version. Try DFU restore.

repair_steps: 1. DFU restore to rule out software
2. If persists: inspect board for signs of heat damage near SoC
3. Reball SoC (advanced micro-soldering, low success rate)
test_procedure: Run a graphics-intensive app (game or benchmark)
Monitor for GPU recovery messages in console
Check for visual artifacts on screen

patterns.title: Kernel data abort — invalid memory access
patterns.diagnosis: The kernel accessed an invalid memory address, causing an immediate panic.

probabilities.description: LPDDR4/5 cell failure. Not repairable.
Flash storage read error propagated to kernel. Try DFU restore first.
Rare. Check if tied to specific iOS version.

repair_steps: 1. DFU restore (rule out NAND filesystem corruption)
3. RAM is part of SoC package — not field-repairable
test_procedure: After DFU restore, use phone normally for 48h

patterns.title: Display/touch service timeout (backboardd)
patterns.diagnosis: backboardd (display + touch input service) stopped responding.

probabilities.description: Damaged or poorly seated display flex. Check FPC connector.
Connector pins bent or corroded on logic board.
Touch controller IC failure. Requires micro-soldering.

repair_steps: 2. Inspect FPC connector under microscope (bent/missing pins)
4. If persists with good display: Meson IC or board issue
test_procedure: Touch responsiveness test (draw app, all screen areas)

patterns.title: WiFi/Bluetooth service failure (wifid)
patterns.diagnosis: wifid stopped responding, indicating WiFi/BT chip communication failure.

probabilities.description: USI 339S00648 or similar. Common after water damage.
WiFi antenna cable not seated or damaged.
Try network settings reset, then DFU restore.

test_procedure: WiFi: connect to 2.4GHz and 5GHz networks, speed test
Check Settings > General > About > WiFi Address (if 'N/A' = chip dead)

patterns.title: Media services failure (mediaserverd)
patterns.diagnosis: mediaserverd hung — handles audio/video playback. Often related to audio IC or codec.

probabilities.description: Audio codec IC unresponsive. Common on iPhone 7/7+ (audio IC disease).
Audio output flex not seated. Reseat connections.
Try DFU restore first.

repair_steps: 2. Reseat speaker and earpiece flex cables
test_procedure: Check if Voice Memos app opens without freezing

patterns.title: Location service failure (locationd)
patterns.diagnosis: locationd hung — handles GPS/location. May indicate GPS antenna or baseband issue.

probabilities.description: GPS antenna cable not seated. Check connections.
GPS is handled by baseband IC. If no signal either, baseband fault.
Reset location services, try DFU restore.

repair_steps: 4. If persists with no cellular signal: baseband IC issue
test_procedure: Open Maps app — does location pin appear?

patterns.title: SpringBoard (home screen) failure
patterns.diagnosis: SpringBoard stopped responding. Usually software-related but can indicate memory issues.

probabilities.description: Filesystem or app cache corruption. DFU restore usually fixes.
Device nearly full, SpringBoard can't cache. Free up space.
If frequent after DFU restore, may indicate RAM failure.

repair_steps: 1. Force restart (volume up, volume down, hold power)
2. Free storage space if nearly full
4. If persists after restore: suspect RAM (board swap)
test_procedure: Open 10+ apps rapidly, switch between them
Check storage: Settings > General > iPhone Storage

patterns.title: Secure Enclave Processor panic
patterns.diagnosis: The SEP crashed. This is a critical, board-level issue.

probabilities.description: SEP is part of the SoC. Not field-repairable.
Power rail issue affecting SEP.

repair_steps: 1. DFU restore (very unlikely to help but worth trying)
2. Board swap — SEP is fused to SoC, cannot be replaced independently
3. Device may be unrepairable if paired components are involved

patterns.title: Memory pressure — app killed by Jetsam
patterns.diagnosis: iOS killed a process due to memory pressure. Usually normal behavior under heavy multitasking.

probabilities.description: Heavy app usage, many background processes. Not a hardware issue.
A specific app consuming excessive memory.
If Jetsam events are extremely frequent (100+/day), may indicate bad RAM cells.

repair_steps: 1. Check frequency — occasional Jetsam is normal
2. If excessive: identify the largest process in the log
4. If 100+ events/day: DFU restore, then suspect RAM if persists
test_procedure: Note which apps trigger the most events
Compare to baseline (5-20/day is normal under active use)

patterns.title: Baseband/cellular modem crash
patterns.diagnosis: The cellular modem firmware crashed. Device may show 'No Service' or 'Searching'.

probabilities.description: Qualcomm/Intel modem IC soldering issue or failure.
Baseband power management IC not supplying correct voltage.
Carrier settings or iOS baseband firmware corruption.

repair_steps: 1. Check for 'No Service' or missing IMEI (Settings > General > About)
4. If IMEI missing: baseband IC or BBPMU replacement (micro-soldering)
test_procedure: Check IMEI in Settings > General > About (if 'Unknown' = baseband dead)
Insert known-good SIM, check for cellular signal
Check if airplane mode toggle works

patterns.title: NAND storage controller panic (ANS2)
patterns.diagnosis: The Apple NAND Storage v2 controller lost communication with flash storage. Critical storage issue.

probabilities.description: Flash storage IC failing. May need NAND replacement.
SoC-NAND communication line fault. Board-level issue.
Severe corruption. DFU restore may recover.

repair_steps: 1. DFU restore (may fail if NAND is physically damaged)
2. If restore fails: NAND chip replacement (expert micro-soldering)
3. If NAND replaced: reprogram with original serial numbers
test_procedure: DFU restore attempt — does it complete?
After restore: write large file, verify data integrity
Check storage capacity: Settings > General > iPhone Storage

patterns.title: SMC panic assertion failure (iPhone 13+)
patterns.diagnosis: System Management Controller assertion failed. Common on iPhone 13+ when sensor arrays report faults.

probabilities.description: One or more sensor flex cables not seated. Check all connections.
Screen replacement missing sensor data. Use OEM or quality aftermarket with sensor transfer.
SMC IC or power rail fault. Advanced repair.

repair_steps: 1. Check all flex cable connections (display, battery, camera, charging)
2. If aftermarket screen: transfer OEM sensor flex or use compatible screen
3. Decode sensor array code in panic log to identify specific sensor
4. If all connections good: SMC issue, board-level repair
test_procedure: Check all sensors: proximity, ALS, microphones, barometer
Monitor for panic recurrence after reseating all flexes

patterns.title: Sleep/Wake hang — audio codec or power issue
patterns.diagnosis: Device hung during sleep/wake transition. Often related to audio IC, amplifier, or power management.

probabilities.description: Audio codec not responding during sleep transition. iPhone 7 'audio IC disease'.
PMU not handling sleep states properly.
Backlight driver not responding during wake.

repair_steps: 2. Test audio: Voice Memos, speakerphone, headphones
3. If audio issues present: audio IC replacement (micro-soldering)
4. If no audio issues: check backlight IC and PMU
test_procedure: Lock phone, wait 5 min, unlock — any delay?
Voice Memos recording during sleep/wake cycles

patterns.title: Audio IC / codec failure
patterns.diagnosis: The Cirrus Logic audio codec IC is failing. Known as 'audio IC disease' on iPhone 7/7+.

probabilities.description: Cold solder joints under audio IC. Very common on iPhone 7. Reball required.
Pads lifted from board. Jumper wire repair needed.
IC itself failed. Full IC replacement.

repair_steps: 1. Test: does Voice Memos app open? (frozen = classic audio IC symptom)
3. Reball audio IC (remove, clean pads, reball, resolder)
4. If pads damaged: jumper wire repair to restore connections
test_procedure: Open Voice Memos — does it freeze/crash?
Speakerphone call — does speaker toggle work?
Record video — is there audio in playback?
Headphones: does audio output work via jack/lightning?

patterns.title: Tristar/Hydra USB charging IC failure
patterns.diagnosis: The Tristar (CBTL1612) or Hydra USB controller IC failed. Causes charging issues, 'This accessory may not be supported'.

probabilities.description: Tristar corroded from liquid exposure. IC replacement required.
IC degraded from using non-MFi cables. Replacement needed.
Lightning port dirty or damaged. Try flex replacement first.

test_procedure: Charge with MFi cable — does it charge?
Check for 'This accessory may not be supported' popup
Connect to iTunes/Finder — does computer recognize device?
Check charging current with USB ammeter

patterns.title: Face ID / TrueDepth camera system failure
patterns.diagnosis: The TrueDepth camera system (dot projector, flood illuminator, or IR camera) has failed.

probabilities.description: Dot projector laser module failed. Not individually replaceable on most models.
IR LED flood illuminator not functioning.
TrueDepth flex cable damaged during screen replacement.
VCSEL driver IC on board failed.

repair_steps: 1. Check if Face ID was working before last repair
4. On iPhone 12+: TrueDepth module can be transferred to new screen
5. If module damaged: Face ID non-functional (no repair, device still usable)
test_procedure: Settings > Face ID & Passcode > Set Up Face ID
Front camera: does Portrait mode work?
Animoji: do face tracking animations work?
Check for 'Face ID is not available' error in Settings

patterns.title: NFC controller failure
patterns.diagnosis: The NFC controller IC (NXP) is not responding. Apple Pay and NFC will not work.

probabilities.description: NXP NFC controller IC failed. Micro-soldering replacement.
NFC antenna coil disconnected or damaged.
DFU restore may resolve.

test_procedure: Apple Pay: try adding a card
NFC tag: hold an NFC tag to back of phone
Express Transit: test with transit card if available

patterns.title: Ultra-Wideband (U1/U2) chip failure
patterns.diagnosis: The UWB chip is not responding. AirDrop direction finding and Precision Finding for AirTags won't work.

probabilities.description: U1/U2 chip failed. Not practically repairable.
UWB antenna cable not seated.
DFU restore may resolve.

repair_steps: 2. Check UWB antenna connections (near top of device)
3. If persists: UWB chip failure — device still functional without precision finding
test_procedure: AirDrop: test with nearby device — does direction arrow appear?
Find My: test Precision Finding with an AirTag
Nearby Interactions: test with another iPhone 11+

patterns.title: Camera ISP (Image Signal Processor) panic
patterns.diagnosis: The camera ISP crashed. Camera may show black screen or freeze the camera app.

probabilities.description: Camera flex cable damaged. Replace rear camera module.
Camera FPC not properly connected after repair.
ISP is part of SoC. DFU restore may help if software-related.

repair_steps: 4. If persists with good camera: ISP/SoC issue (board swap)
test_procedure: Open Camera app — does viewfinder show?
Take photo with each camera (wide, ultra-wide, telephoto)
Record video — does it complete without freezing?

patterns.title: Thermal shutdown — excessive temperature
patterns.diagnosis: Device shut down due to excessive temperature. May indicate cooling system or battery issue.

probabilities.description: Direct sunlight, hot car, charging while gaming. Not a defect.
Old or defective battery causing excess heat. Replace battery.
Component on board drawing excess current, generating heat.
Thermal interface material dried out (older devices).

repair_steps: 1. Check battery health (Settings > Battery > Battery Health)
3. Check for board-level shorts with thermal camera or DC power supply
4. If specific area hot: identify and replace faulty component
test_procedure: Use phone normally for 1h — does it get excessively hot?
Charge phone — is charging area extremely hot?
Use thermal camera to identify hot spots on board

patterns.title: Power Management Unit (PMU) failure
patterns.diagnosis: The PMU/PMIC chip is not functioning correctly. Causes boot issues, random shutdowns, or no power.

probabilities.description: PMU chip (Dialog/Apple) failed. Micro-soldering replacement.
Short circuit on a power rail. Identify and fix shorted component.
Battery not providing correct voltage. Try new battery.

repair_steps: 1. Check boot current with DC power supply
3. Identify shorted rails with thermal camera
test_procedure: Boot current pattern (normal is spike then settle)
Check all voltage rails with multimeter

patterns.title: Display backlight / brightness IC failure
patterns.diagnosis: The backlight driver IC failed. Screen may appear very dim or have no backlight.

probabilities.description: Backlight driver IC failed. Micro-soldering replacement.
Backlight filter on board blown (often from connecting screen while battery is connected).
Backlight lines in display flex damaged.

repair_steps: 1. Test: shine flashlight at screen — can you see image? (backlight dead but display works)
3. Replace backlight IC if filter is good
4. Try known-good display to rule out flex damage
test_procedure: Flashlight test: shine light through screen, look for faint image
Connect to computer — does iTunes/Finder detect device?

patterns.title: I2C/SPI bus communication failure
patterns.diagnosis: Inter-chip communication bus failed. Multiple sensors/ICs may be affected.

probabilities.description: Corrosion on bus lines. Board cleaning and trace repair.
Drop damage causing broken traces.
A device on the I2C/SPI bus pulling lines low.

repair_steps: 1. Inspect board under microscope for corrosion/damage
2. Clean board with IPA and ultrasonic cleaner
4. Repair broken traces or replace faulty ICs
test_procedure: Check which functions are not working (often multiple)
Test: touch, sensors, camera, WiFi systematically

patterns.title: App crash — memory access violation (EXC_BAD_ACCESS)
patterns.diagnosis: The app accessed an invalid memory address. Usually a software bug (null pointer, use-after-free), but if it affects many different apps it may indicate RAM issues.

probabilities.description: Most common. Update or reinstall the app.
If many unrelated apps crash with SIGSEGV, suspect soldered RAM failure.
Known iOS bug in specific version. Check for iOS update.

repair_steps: 1. Update or reinstall the crashing app
3. If many different apps affected: backup + restore iPhone
4. If persists after restore: suspect RAM failure — professional board repair needed
test_procedure: Reproduce crash in the specific app
Test same operation in other apps
Check if crash disappears after iOS update

patterns.title: App crash — assertion failure (SIGABRT)
patterns.diagnosis: The app called abort() due to an assertion failure or unhandled exception. This is almost always a software issue in the app itself.

probabilities.cause: App bug — unhandled exception or failed assertion
probabilities.description: Developer error. Update or reinstall the app.
App state corrupted. Offload and reinstall the app.

repair_steps: 1. Update the app to latest version
2. Offload and reinstall the app (Settings > General > iPhone Storage)
3. Contact app developer if issue persists

patterns.title: App terminated — memory limit exceeded
patterns.diagnosis: The app was killed by iOS because it exceeded its memory allocation. Normal for memory-heavy apps; persistent cases may indicate a memory leak.

probabilities.description: The app is consuming too much RAM. Update or reinstall.

probabilities.cause: Device under memory pressure (too many apps open)
probabilities.description: Close background apps and test again.
App requires more RAM than available on this model.

repair_steps: 2. Close background apps before using it
test_procedure: Open app after fresh restart with no background apps

patterns.title: Memory pressure — system killed background apps (Jetsam)
patterns.diagnosis: iOS killed background apps to free RAM. This is normal behavior. Very frequent events may indicate insufficient RAM for the workload or an app with a memory leak.

probabilities.description: iOS kills background apps when foreground apps need RAM. Not a problem.
A specific app is consuming too much RAM. Identify and update it.
Very old iPhones may show degraded RAM performance over time.

repair_steps: 1. Check which app triggers most jetsam events (Largest Process field)
test_procedure: Note the 'largest process' in the crash details
Test device performance after closing that app

patterns.title: GPU hang — graphics processor lockup
patterns.diagnosis: The GPU locked up and required a forced restart. Isolated events may be software-related; repeated events suggest a hardware GPU or thermal issue.

probabilities.cause: GPU driver bug / app pushing GPU too hard
probabilities.description: Update iOS and the app causing GPU stress.
Device overheating causes GPU errors. Check thermal events.
GPU silicon defect or solder joint failure. Board repair required.

repair_steps: 2. Update the app triggering GPU events
3. Ensure device is not overheating during use
4. If frequent: professional board-level GPU repair
test_procedure: Test GPU-intensive tasks (games, video) after cooling device
Check for thermal events in the same time period

patterns.title: OTA/restore failure — possible hardware issue
patterns.diagnosis: The device failed during an OTA update or restore. Error 4013/4014/9 typically indicate a hardware problem with the dock connector, NAND chip, or logic board.

probabilities.description: Faulty cable or dock connector causing communication failure. Try original cable.
NAND chip or controller failing. Board repair required.
Baseband firmware update failed. Board repair.
Try different USB port, cable, and computer.

repair_steps: 1. Try with original Apple cable on a different USB port
test_procedure: Check error code in OTA log

patterns.title: Thermal event — device temperature management
patterns.diagnosis: iOS logged a thermal event to manage device temperature. Isolated events are normal during heavy workloads. Frequent events at idle may indicate a hardware thermal issue.

probabilities.description: Expected during gaming, video recording, charging. Not a problem.
Identify the process in thermal logs and update/replace it.

probabilities.cause: Thermal sensor or cooling system fault
probabilities.description: Defective thermal sensor or damaged heat spreader. Hardware repair.

repair_steps: 1. Identify if thermal events occur during specific activities
2. Ensure device is not in a case that traps heat
3. Check for app consuming high CPU in the background
4. If device is hot at idle: suspect hardware thermal sensor issue
test_procedure: Monitor device temperature during idle vs load
Check which process is listed in thermal logs

patterns.title: Watchdog timeout — system daemon did not respond
patterns.diagnosis: A critical iOS system daemon failed to respond within the watchdog timeout, forcing a reboot. The specific daemon indicates which subsystem failed.

probabilities.description: iOS software issue. Update iOS.

probabilities.cause: Hardware component served by that daemon is failing
probabilities.description: If the daemon manages hardware (audio, display, WiFi), the underlying hardware may be failing.
Device running too many processes. Restart and reduce load.

repair_steps: 2. Backup and restore iPhone via Finder/iTunes
3. Check the specific daemon — it indicates the failing subsystem
test_procedure: Cross-reference with other crash types in same time period

patterns.title: EXC_BAD_ACCESS — invalid memory address dereference
patterns.diagnosis: App tried to read/write a null or freed pointer. Classic use-after-free or dangling pointer bug.

probabilities.description: Object released while still referenced.
Pointer never assigned before dereference.
Buffer overflow overwrote return address.

repair_steps: Check recent Xcode updates for known bugs
Reinstall app — may clear corrupted state

patterns.title: EXC_BAD_ACCESS — memory protection violation
patterns.diagnosis: App attempted to write to read-only memory or execute non-executable memory. Often a code signing or JIT issue.

probabilities.description: App modified a constant or code segment.
Unsigned executable pages blocked by kernel.
JavaScript engine failed to map RWX pages.

test_procedure: Try older iOS if jailbroken env

patterns.title: SIGILL — illegal instruction / code signing failure
patterns.diagnosis: CPU encountered an invalid instruction. Often bad code signing, corrupted binary, or attempted execution of non-arm64 code.

probabilities.description: Download corrupted — reinstall fixes.
Provisioning profile mismatch or revoked.
x86 code attempted on arm64.

repair_steps: Clear iOS cache (Settings > General > iPhone Storage)

patterns.title: EXC_ARITHMETIC — division by zero or FP exception
patterns.diagnosis: Arithmetic exception: division by zero, integer overflow with trap, or floating-point exception.

probabilities.description: Denominator is zero in integer or FP division.
Swift/C integer overflow with trapping arithmetic.
NaN or infinity produced in FP operation.

test_procedure: Trigger specific app flow that leads to crash

patterns.title: EXC_BREAKPOINT — Swift runtime trap
patterns.diagnosis: Swift runtime detected a fatal condition: force-unwrap of nil, array out of bounds, integer overflow. Swift inserts trap instructions for these.

probabilities.description: app! on nil value → immediate trap.
Swift bounds-checking trap.
+ operator with overflow detection.

repair_steps: Report to developer with crash log
test_procedure: Reproduce specific user action before crash

patterns.title: Swift fatal error — explicit fatalError() or precondition failure
patterns.diagnosis: App called fatalError(), preconditionFailure(), or assert() with an unrecoverable condition.

probabilities.description: Developer explicitly called fatalError for nil scenario.
precondition() or assert() violated.
fatalError('not implemented') stub reached.

repair_steps: Clear app data if state corruption suspected

patterns.title: EXC_RESOURCE — CPU time limit exceeded
patterns.diagnosis: App consumed too much CPU time. iOS killed it to protect battery and system responsiveness.

probabilities.description: Background task stuck in loop.
Heavy processing without yielding.
Timers accumulating without cancellation.

repair_steps: Check Battery settings for excessive background CPU

patterns.title: EXC_GUARD — guarded file descriptor violation
patterns.diagnosis: App violated a guarded file descriptor — attempted to close or manipulate a system-protected fd (socket, pipe).

probabilities.description: App closed a protected file descriptor it didn't own.
File descriptor closed twice.
Unsupported operation on guarded resource.

test_procedure: Trigger network/file operation that causes crash

patterns.title: Watchdog kill 0x8badf00d — app failed to launch/resume in time
patterns.diagnosis: SpringBoard killed app for taking too long to launch, resume from background, or respond to system event. 'Bad food' — the classic iOS watchdog code.

probabilities.description: App doing disk/network read on main thread during startup.
Lock contention blocks main thread.
Parsing/decoding large data on main thread.

repair_steps: Clear app cache in Settings > General > iPhone Storage
test_procedure: Launch app cold — does it hang before showing UI?

patterns.title: 0xdead10cc — app suspended while holding file lock
patterns.diagnosis: App was suspended by iOS while holding a file system lock. iOS killed it to prevent system deadlock.

probabilities.description: Database transaction open when app went to background.
App opened file exclusively and didn't close before suspend.
App group shared container locked by extension.

test_procedure: Background app immediately after database operation
Check if specific workflow triggers crash

patterns.title: abort() / assertion failure — C runtime abort
patterns.diagnosis: C/C++ assertion macro fired or explicit abort() called. Common in lower-level libraries (OpenSSL, SQLite, etc.).

probabilities.description: Unhandled Objective-C exception propagated to C layer.
assert(condition) evaluated false.
libsystem_malloc detected heap corruption.

patterns.title: EXC_RESOURCE — memory limit exceeded (hard cap)
patterns.diagnosis: App hit its per-process memory hard limit. iOS sent SIGKILL after the limit was exceeded.

probabilities.description: Heap growing unboundedly due to retain cycles.
Uncompressed media buffered in memory.
In-memory cache without eviction policy.

repair_steps: Close other apps to free RAM before using

patterns.title: Kernel double-free — heap corruption in kernel
patterns.diagnosis: Kernel attempted to free a memory block that was already freed. Indicates a kernel bug or corrupted kernel heap.

probabilities.description: Third-party or Apple driver with reference counting error.
Concurrent free operations without proper locking.
Bit flip in DRAM causing metadata corruption.

test_procedure: Check iOS version — often fixed in point releases

patterns.title: Kernel use-after-free — dangling pointer in kernel
patterns.diagnosis: Kernel code accessed a memory object after it was freed. Classic memory safety bug in kernel driver.

probabilities.description: IOKit object released with active references remaining.
Object freed while interrupt handler uses it.
Memory corruption triggering false UAF pattern.

test_procedure: Check iOS update history for relevant fixes

patterns.title: Kernel stack overflow — kernel thread exhausted stack
patterns.diagnosis: A kernel thread overflowed its stack, triggering the stack canary/guard protection. Often deep recursion in a kernel driver.

probabilities.description: Recursive callback loop in I/O driver.
IRQ handler calling functions with large stack frames.
KEXT with excessive stack allocation.

repair_steps: Remove recently installed profiles or MDM config

patterns.title: Bus alignment fault — unaligned memory access in kernel
patterns.diagnosis: Kernel code accessed memory at an unaligned address. ARM64 requires certain types to be naturally aligned.

probabilities.description: Packed struct member access causing misalignment.
Off-by-one error producing unaligned address.
Hardware DMA descriptor not page-aligned.

test_procedure: Note specific hardware interaction before panic

patterns.title: Undefined instruction — invalid opcode executed in kernel
patterns.diagnosis: CPU encountered an undefined/reserved instruction while executing kernel code. Indicates memory corruption or wrong binary.

probabilities.description: Code segment overwritten with data, producing invalid opcodes.
KEXT compiled for different CPU revision.
Function pointer corruption jumps to non-code.

patterns.title: WMR_PANIC — wireless mobile router subsystem panic
patterns.diagnosis: Panic in the WirelessMobileRouter kernel component, typically during Personal Hotspot or WiFi operations.

probabilities.description: Race condition in WiFi/BT coexistence layer.
Personal Hotspot entering invalid state.
BCM4389 or similar chip assertion.

patterns.title: H2FMI NAND controller panic — flash storage hardware failure
patterns.diagnosis: H2FMI (Host-to-Flash Memory Interface) controller panicked. Indicates NAND flash storage failure or controller hardware issue.

probabilities.description: Flash memory block/chip reaching end of life.
H2FMI controller IC damage, often from water.
Solder joint failure on NAND package.

repair_steps: Replace NAND/logic board — requires microsoldering or board swap
This is a hardware failure requiring professional repair
test_procedure: Check if device boots after DFU — if NAND fails DFU restore will error 4013/4014
File Diagnostics will show storage errors

patterns.title: AMCC panic — Apple Memory Cache Controller failure
patterns.diagnosis: AMCC (Apple Memory Cache Controller / SoC memory controller) panicked. Often indicates DRAM or SoC-level hardware fault.

probabilities.description: RAM chip or solder ball failure causing memory error.
Firmware bug in memory controller — fixed by iOS update.
Overheating causing memory controller instability.

repair_steps: If hardware: logic board replacement required
test_procedure: Stress test with heavy app usage

patterns.title: AppleNAND panic — NAND flash storage critical error
patterns.diagnosis: AppleNAND driver detected an unrecoverable flash storage error. High likelihood of hardware storage failure.

probabilities.description: Flash cells exhausted — common in old or heavily used devices.
ANS2 or storage controller IC failure.
Bad block map overflow — flash management layer failing.

repair_steps: Replace storage chip (microsoldering) or full logic board
DFU restore may fail with error 4013
test_procedure: Attempt DFU restore — error 4013/4014 confirms NAND failure

patterns.title: VM fault panic — virtual memory paging failure
patterns.diagnosis: Kernel VM fault handler encountered an error it couldn't recover from. Usually a page-in failure due to I/O error or corruption.

probabilities.description: Paging from NAND failed due to storage error.
VM compressor state machine error.
vm_map zone depleted.

patterns.title: Translation fault — MMU page table failure
patterns.diagnosis: ARM64 MMU encountered a translation fault: page table entry missing or invalid for a kernel virtual address.

probabilities.cause: Kernel pointer out of mapped range
probabilities.description: Kernel code dereferenced an unmapped virtual address.
DRAM error corrupted kernel page table entry.
IOKit driver accessed freed IOMemoryMap.

patterns.title: Permission fault — kernel accessed restricted memory region
patterns.diagnosis: ARM64 MMU blocked kernel access to a memory region due to page permission bits. Indicates a security violation or kernel bug.

probabilities.description: Attempt to write to kernel text region protected by KTRR.
Kernel read/wrote user memory without proper mapping.
Execute-never page executed by kernel.

repair_steps: Update iOS — this is typically a kernel security fix
test_procedure: Check iOS security advisories for related CVE

patterns.title: Synchronous external abort — SoC bus error
patterns.diagnosis: ARM64 SError (System Error): an asynchronous abort from the memory system. Typically hardware: DRAM ECC error, bus fabric fault, or PMU error.

probabilities.description: Uncorrectable DRAM bit flip.
AXI/APB bus fabric signal integrity issue.
Supply voltage drop causing SoC bus error.

repair_steps: Check for corrosion/water damage under microscope

patterns.title: Hardware watchdog timeout — kernel failed to kick watchdog
patterns.diagnosis: Hardware watchdog timer fired because the kernel failed to reset it in time. Kernel was hung (deadlock, infinite loop, or hardware hang).

probabilities.description: Spinlock or mutex deadlock preventing kernel from running.
I/O operation stalled waiting for hardware that never responded.
Power management communication stalled.

repair_steps: Check for water damage (hardware hang cause)
Logic board repair if hardware cause
test_procedure: Force restart and check if reproduces

patterns.title: Spinlock timeout — kernel lock held too long
patterns.diagnosis: A kernel spinlock was held longer than the watchdog timeout allows. Indicates a deadlock or a thread holding a lock while waiting for hardware.

probabilities.description: Two drivers waiting for each other's lock.
Interrupt handler running too long holding a lock.
I/O wait inside spinlock context.

test_procedure: Note specific hardware interaction before panic

patterns.title: Kernel panic — explicit kernel assert/panic call
patterns.diagnosis: Kernel code hit an explicit panic() or Assert() call — a condition the developer deemed unrecoverable.

probabilities.description: Kernel reached an invariant violation — usually fixed in next update.
Earlier memory corruption led to an impossible state.
Bad RAM or power glitch corrupted kernel data structure.

patterns.title: IOKit assertion failure — driver framework error
patterns.diagnosis: IOKit driver framework hit an assertion. Often a driver attempting an operation after the service was terminated.

probabilities.description: Driver called IOService methods after terminate.
Acquire lock in wrong context (interrupt level).
IOKit object released more than retained.

patterns.title: DRAM initialization failure — RAM calibration error
patterns.diagnosis: DRAM controller failed to initialize or calibrate LPDDR memory at boot. Strong hardware indicator — DRAM chip or SoC memory interface failure.

probabilities.description: LPDDR DRAM chip died or partially failed.
PoP solder connection between SoC and DRAM broken.
Memory PHY block in SoC damaged.

repair_steps: Reball/reflow PoP may work in some cases — requires professional
test_procedure: Device will fail to boot past Apple logo

patterns.title: CPU exception in kernel — unhandled exception in kernel mode
patterns.diagnosis: An unhandled CPU exception occurred while executing in kernel mode. Could be SVC, undefined, or exception from privileged code.

probabilities.description: Unexpected exception in kernel code path.
Silicon bug in specific CPU revision.

probabilities.cause: Memory corruption leading to bad execution
probabilities.description: Corrupted code pointer executed.

test_procedure: Check iOS version for known CPU errata fixes

patterns.title: Kernel page fault — unexpected page fault in kernel mode
patterns.diagnosis: Kernel code generated a page fault that couldn't be resolved. Often a NULL pointer dereference in a kernel driver.

probabilities.description: Driver accessed a struct member through NULL pointer.
Kernel accessed a page that was unmapped/freed.
Address calculation error in driver.

patterns.title: Jetsam: per-process memory limit exceeded
patterns.diagnosis: Process exceeded its per-process memory limit set by iOS. Background apps have stricter limits than foreground.

probabilities.description: App gradually leaking memory until limit hit.
App loaded too much data into memory at once.
Image cache growing without NSCache eviction.

repair_steps: Restart device to clear memory pressure
test_procedure: Monitor memory in Settings > General > iPhone Storage

patterns.title: Jetsam: high-water mark exceeded
patterns.diagnosis: Process memory allocation exceeded the high-water mark warning threshold before hitting the hard limit.

probabilities.description: App memory slowly growing over time without cleanup.
Background refresh growing memory beyond threshold.
UIKit/WebKit memory not released after view dismissal.

test_procedure: Use app for extended period — does memory grow?

patterns.title: Jetsam: VM page shortage — system-wide memory pressure
patterns.diagnosis: System ran out of free VM pages, forcing jetsam to kill processes to free memory.

probabilities.description: Multiple large apps consuming available pages.
Several apps leaking memory simultaneously.
System framework allocated large buffer (Photos, Mail).

patterns.title: Jetsam: idle exit — normal background app cleanup
patterns.diagnosis: App was idle in background and iOS reclaimed its memory. This is normal iOS behavior, not a crash.

probabilities.description: iOS reclaimed idle app memory as expected.

probabilities.cause: App requested background time but wasn't used
probabilities.description: Background task acquired but app became idle.

repair_steps: No action needed — this is expected behavior

patterns.title: Jetsam: VM compressor space shortage
patterns.diagnosis: iOS VM compressor (the swap replacement) ran out of space. All compressed memory slots are full, forcing jetsam kills.

probabilities.description: So many apps open that even compressed swap is full.
Data not compressing well (already compressed content).
Single large app filling compressor slots.

test_procedure: Restart resolves — if chronic, check for memory-leaking apps

patterns.title: Jetsam: kernel zone map exhaustion
patterns.diagnosis: Kernel memory zone map (used for kernel object allocation) ran out of space. Can cause cascading panics or jetsam.

probabilities.description: Driver allocating from a zone without freeing.

probabilities.cause: App opening too many file descriptors
probabilities.description: vnode/socket zone exhausted by many open files.
App creating Mach ports without releasing them.

patterns.title: memorystatus_kill — iOS memory status daemon forced kill
patterns.diagnosis: memorystatus daemon killed the process as part of pressure relief. Priority-based: background apps killed before foreground.

probabilities.description: System memory critically low, background apps killed.
Current app exceeded its memory budget.
System daemon exceeded its memory allocation.

repair_steps: Restart device to free all memory

patterns.title: Jetsam: sustained critical memory pressure
patterns.diagnosis: Device remained in critical memory pressure state for an extended period, triggering aggressive jetsam.

probabilities.description: Game or media app consuming large memory constantly.
Several apps slowly growing their memory footprint.
Device RAM insufficient for current tasks.

repair_steps: Consider upgrading to device with more RAM

patterns.title: Jetsam: foreground app killed for memory
patterns.diagnosis: The frontmost (currently active) app was killed for memory. This is unusual — foreground apps get the most memory headroom.

probabilities.description: App exceeded the absolute per-process memory ceiling.
Uncompressed textures or large media loaded.
Leak so severe it triggered foreground kill.

test_procedure: Reproduce specific action (load large image, open large file)

patterns.title: Jetsam: kernel wired page shortage
patterns.diagnosis: Kernel wired memory (memory that cannot be paged or compressed) grew too large, leaving insufficient free pages.

probabilities.description: Driver allocated and wired large DMA buffer without releasing.
GPU surfaces wired by graphics stack accumulating.
Process with many tiny mappings causing kernel overhead.

test_procedure: Check if specific app triggers it

patterns.title: Watchdog: bluetoothd hung or unresponsive
patterns.diagnosis: Bluetooth daemon failed to respond to watchdog within timeout. Causes device respring or reboot.

probabilities.description: BCM chip firmware stopped responding to host.
Bluetooth service deadlocked on internal lock.
Connected accessory causing daemon loop.

repair_steps: Forget all Bluetooth devices and re-pair
test_procedure: Remove paired devices one by one

patterns.title: Watchdog: nfcd NFC daemon hung
patterns.diagnosis: NFC daemon failed to respond to watchdog. May indicate NFC chip communication failure or daemon deadlock.

probabilities.description: NFC controller IC not responding to daemon.
NFC service stuck waiting for chip response.
Wallet transaction state causing daemon hang.

repair_steps: Reset Wallet if Apple Pay related
NFC chip replacement if hardware failure

patterns.title: Watchdog: biometrickitd biometric daemon hung
patterns.diagnosis: BiometricKit daemon unresponsive. Usually related to Touch ID or Face ID hardware communication failure.

probabilities.description: Biometric sensor not responding to daemon requests.
Secure Enclave Processor not responding to biometric requests.
Daemon stuck in lock contention.

test_procedure: Test Touch ID/Face ID — does it fail?

patterns.title: Watchdog: fairplayd DRM daemon hung
patterns.diagnosis: FairPlay DRM daemon failed to respond. Affects App Store purchases, Apple TV, Music, and DRM-protected content.

probabilities.description: DRM service stuck during license validation.
SEP key extraction timeout during FairPlay operation.
Network timeout during license renewal causing daemon hang.

repair_steps: Sign out and back into Apple ID
test_procedure: Sign out of Apple ID and back in

patterns.title: Watchdog: trustd certificate/trust daemon hung
patterns.diagnosis: trustd (certificate trust evaluation daemon) failed to respond. Affects TLS, app validation, and certificate verification.

probabilities.description: trustd stuck validating a certificate chain.
Certificate revocation check timing out.
trustd waiting for securityd/keychain.

repair_steps: Check date/time settings (trust failures when clock wrong)

patterns.title: Watchdog: accessoryd Lightning/USB accessory daemon hung
patterns.diagnosis: Accessory daemon unresponsive. Usually triggered by a MFi accessory communication failure or Lightning/USB-C port issue.

probabilities.description: Non-genuine or damaged accessory causing daemon loop.
Debris/corrosion causing spurious accessory signals.
USB port controller IC causing communication errors.

test_procedure: Disconnect all accessories — does reboot stop?

patterns.title: Watchdog: powerd power management daemon hung
patterns.diagnosis: Power management daemon unresponsive. Affects sleep/wake, battery management, thermal management, and power states.

probabilities.description: powerd blocked waiting for PMU response.
Thermal event handling deadlock.
Battery gas gauge not responding.

repair_steps: Check battery health — replace if below 80%

patterns.title: Watchdog: notifyd IPC notification daemon hung
patterns.diagnosis: Darwin notification daemon failed to respond. notifyd handles inter-process notifications via notify_post(). Its hang blocks many system services.

probabilities.description: Too many pending notifications or dead subscribers.
Daemon locked waiting for a subscriber that is also waiting.
App posting notifications too rapidly.

repair_steps: Update apps that may post excessive notifications
test_procedure: Check if specific app is posting many notifications

patterns.title: Watchdog: dasd disk arbitration daemon hung
patterns.diagnosis: Disk arbitration daemon unresponsive. dasd handles device mounting/unmounting and storage policy. Its hang can prevent file system operations.

probabilities.description: NAND I/O operation stalling the daemon.
Deadlock with APFS or file system daemon.
Extremely low free space causing I/O errors.

repair_steps: DFU restore if storage hardware issue

patterns.title: Watchdog: CarPlay/external display daemon hung
patterns.diagnosis: CarPlay or external display agent failed to respond. Can cause device respring when connecting to car or display.

probabilities.description: Head unit sending unexpected CarPlay protocol messages.
Hardware video encoder stalled during CarPlay display setup.
USB-C DisplayPort alt mode negotiation timeout.

test_procedure: Disconnect from car — does crash stop?
Update iOS and head unit firmware

patterns.title: AGX firmware panic — GPU firmware crashed
patterns.diagnosis: Apple AGX GPU firmware encountered an unrecoverable error. Can be software (driver bug, shader error) or hardware (GPU damage).

probabilities.description: Malformed Metal shader causing GPU fault.
Race between CPU and GPU command submission.
GPU core damage from heat or electrical event.

patterns.title: Metal GPU out of memory — shader or buffer allocation failed
patterns.diagnosis: Metal framework ran out of GPU-accessible memory. Either shader compilation OOM or buffer allocation failure.

probabilities.description: App or game allocated excessive GPU buffers.
Shader compiled at runtime during memory pressure.
High-resolution textures exceeding GPU memory.

patterns.title: IOSurface / framebuffer panic — display buffer management failure
patterns.diagnosis: IOSurface or IOMobileFramebuffer driver encountered a fatal error. Manages shared memory between CPU/GPU for display.

probabilities.description: Surface freed while still referenced by display.
DMA transfer error to display controller.
Cross-process IOSurface invalidated mid-transfer.

test_procedure: Check if specific app triggers the panic

patterns.title: Display pipe / DCP panic — display controller failure
patterns.diagnosis: Apple Display Controller Processor (DCP) or display pipe panicked. Can indicate hardware damage or driver bug.

probabilities.description: Display cable causing intermittent display pipeline errors.
Display controller software bug — fixed in iOS update.
Display timing controller hardware failure.

repair_steps: Check if display is original or replaced
Reseat display connector if recently repaired
test_procedure: Press on screen corners — any flickering?

patterns.title: HDCP authentication failure — content protection error
patterns.diagnosis: HDCP (High-bandwidth Digital Content Protection) authentication failed when connecting to an external display or TV.

probabilities.description: Adapter or TV doesn't support required HDCP version.
Device's HDCP keys corrupted or expired.
HDCP handshake timing issue in DCP firmware.

test_procedure: Try Apple Lightning Digital AV Adapter

patterns.title: AGX G14/G15/G16 GPU generation-specific panic
patterns.diagnosis: Panic specific to Apple's 14th/15th/16th generation AGX GPU (iPhone 14/15/16 series). Usually a firmware-specific bug.

probabilities.cause: AGX firmware bug for this GPU generation
probabilities.description: Known issue fixed in iOS point release.
Shader exploiting GPU-specific behavior.

probabilities.cause: Hardware defect on specific GPU die
probabilities.description: Manufacturing defect in GPU cores.

test_procedure: Update iOS — check release notes for GPU fixes

patterns.title: GPU IOMMU / DART fault — DMA protection violation
patterns.diagnosis: Apple DART (Device Address Resolution Table) — the GPU IOMMU — blocked an invalid DMA transaction from the GPU.

probabilities.description: Driver programmed GPU to DMA to an unmapped address.
Firmware-level DMA transaction exceeding mapped region.
GPU still accessing IOSurface after CPU freed it.

patterns.title: ProMotion variable refresh rate fault
patterns.diagnosis: Panic related to ProMotion (120Hz adaptive refresh) display timing controller. Usually a driver issue.

probabilities.description: Race condition in adaptive refresh rate controller.
Animation mismatched with display refresh timing.
OLED/LTPO panel timing controller issue.

repair_steps: Disable ProMotion temporarily: Settings > Accessibility > Motion

patterns.title: AppleBaseband crash — cellular modem failure
patterns.diagnosis: Apple baseband (cellular modem) crashed and caused a system panic or reset. Can be firmware or hardware.

probabilities.description: Modem firmware hit unhandled edge case — iOS update fixes.
PMB/Qualcomm modem chip hardware fault.
RF frontend fault causing modem exception.

test_procedure: Enable Airplane Mode — does stability improve?

patterns.title: ICE (Intel) modem crash — Intel baseband failure
patterns.diagnosis: Intel XMM-series baseband modem crash. Affects iPhone 7/8/X models with Intel modem variant.

probabilities.description: Known firmware issue — update resolves.
XMM chip hardware defect.
Antenna calibration data corrupted.

test_procedure: Check Settings > General > About > Modem Firmware for Intel indicator

patterns.title: Qualcomm X55/X65 modem crash — 5G modem failure
patterns.diagnosis: Qualcomm X55 or X65 5G baseband modem crash. Affects iPhone 12+ 5G models.

probabilities.description: 5G protocol handling edge case — fixed in updates.
5G mmWave antenna module failure (US models).
SDX55/SDX65 chip hardware fault.

repair_steps: Disable 5G temporarily: Settings > Cellular > Voice & Data > LTE
test_procedure: Switch from 5G to LTE — does stability improve?

patterns.title: SIM card failure — cellular identity module fault
patterns.diagnosis: SIM card or SIM reader hardware failure. Can cause baseband crashes as modem loses cellular identity.

probabilities.description: Physical SIM card chip failed.
Water damage or debris on SIM contacts.
eSIM data corrupted or failed provisioning.

test_procedure: Check for corrosion on SIM tray

patterns.title: RF frontend / antenna failure — signal hardware fault
patterns.diagnosis: RF frontend hardware (antenna switch, PA module, RF calibration IC) failure causing baseband crashes or no service.

probabilities.description: RF antenna switch IC damaged — common after drops.
Cellular PA module hardware fault.
NVRAM calibration values for RF frontend lost.

repair_steps: Logic board inspection for physical damage near RF area
test_procedure: Field Test mode (*3001#12345#*) — check signal RSRP

patterns.title: Baseband firmware update failed — modem update error
patterns.diagnosis: iOS update failed to flash new baseband firmware. Device may get stuck in boot loop or show 'No Service'.

probabilities.cause: NAND storage error during firmware write
probabilities.description: Flash error prevented firmware write.
Baseband NOR flash partition corrupted.
Device lost power mid-update leaving firmware in bad state.

repair_steps: If DFU fails: logic board replacement
test_procedure: Check Settings > General > About > Modem Firmware — is version current?

patterns.title: Baseband reset loop — modem repeatedly crashing
patterns.diagnosis: Baseband modem is crashing and restarting repeatedly, causing continuous reboots or loss of cellular service.

probabilities.description: Baseband firmware partition has bad blocks.
PMB/QCom chip damaged — often from water/power surge.
Modem VCC power rail unstable.

repair_steps: Logic board replacement if DFU fails or hardware confirmed
test_procedure: If still loops after restore: hardware failure

patterns.title: Antenna tuner IC failure — RF tuning hardware fault
patterns.diagnosis: Tunable antenna IC (Qorvo/Skyworks) failure causing RF performance degradation and eventual baseband crash.

probabilities.description: Qorvo/Skyworks tuner IC failed — common after drops.
Physical damage to antenna assembly.
NVRAM calibration values for tuner corrupted.

repair_steps: Inspect for physical damage to antenna assembly
Check cellular reception before and after repair
test_procedure: Field Test mode — check cellular signal strength

patterns.title: SEP firmware panic — Secure Enclave Processor crash
patterns.diagnosis: Secure Enclave Processor firmware panicked. SEP handles all cryptographic keys, Touch ID/Face ID, and secure boot chain.

probabilities.description: Firmware defect in SEP — often fixed in iOS update.
Secure Enclave hardware fault on SoC.
Secure key storage NVM corrupted.

test_procedure: If Touch ID/Face ID fails after panic: DFU restore

patterns.title: Secure Boot failure — boot chain integrity violation
patterns.diagnosis: iOS Secure Boot chain validation failed. Could be a corrupted iOS image, hardware fault, or unauthorized modification.

probabilities.description: Flash corruption in system partition invalidating signatures.
Boot ROM or iBoot partition storage failure.
Jailbreak or unsigned code invalidating boot chain.

repair_steps: If DFU fails: hardware failure on storage/SoC
test_procedure: Put device in DFU mode and restore
If restore fails with error 4013: NAND failure

patterns.title: AMFI code signing failure — unauthorized code execution blocked
patterns.diagnosis: Apple Mobile File Integrity (AMFI) blocked execution of code that failed signature verification.

probabilities.description: App data corrupted in NAND — reinstall fixes.
Developer/enterprise profile expired.
App binary modified by unauthorized tool.

repair_steps: If enterprise app: contact app administrator for re-provisioning

patterns.title: Touch ID SEP communication failure
patterns.diagnosis: Touch ID sensor cannot communicate with SEP. The fingerprint sensor and its matching algorithm run in SEP isolation.

probabilities.cause: Home button replacement with non-genuine part
probabilities.description: Third-party home button loses Touch ID pairing with SEP.
Cable connecting home button to logic board damaged.
SEP unable to authenticate the Touch ID sensor.

repair_steps: Apple repair only — Touch ID is paired to specific SEP, non-Apple replacements disable Touch ID
Check home button cable connection if recently repaired
test_procedure: Test Touch ID enrollment in Settings > Touch ID & Passcode

patterns.title: SEP biometric lockout — too many failed authentication attempts
patterns.diagnosis: SEP locked out biometric authentication after too many failed Touch ID/Face ID attempts, requiring passcode.

probabilities.cause: Multiple failed Touch ID/Face ID attempts
probabilities.description: Normal security lockout after failed attempts.
SEP entered lockout state erroneously.
Accessibility service repeatedly triggering failed auth.

repair_steps: Enter device passcode to re-enable biometrics

patterns.title: SEP firmware update failed — Secure Enclave update error
patterns.diagnosis: iOS OTA or DFU restore failed to update SEP firmware. Device may be bricked or unable to complete restore.

probabilities.cause: NAND flash error during SEP partition write
probabilities.description: Bad flash blocks in SEP firmware partition.
Battery died or cable unplugged during update.
SEP NVM storage hardware failure.

repair_steps: Retry DFU restore with full charge and reliable cable
If fails: logic board replacement required
test_procedure: DFU restore with device connected to computer
Monitor for error codes during restore

patterns.title: PMU SPMI bus assertion — power management communication failure
patterns.diagnosis: Apple PMU driver panicked on an SPMI (System Power Management Interface) bus error. Indicates hardware failure in power delivery subsystem.

probabilities.description: Power Management IC hardware fault.
Corrosion or damage on SPMI traces between SoC and PMIC.
Driver asserting on unexpected PMIC state — iOS update may fix.

repair_steps: Logic board inspection for liquid damage
test_procedure: Check corrosion indicators on logic board
Monitor for thermal events before crash

patterns.title: BMIC / charging IC failure — battery management chip fault
patterns.diagnosis: Battery Management IC (BMIC, BQ25xxx family) failure. Controls charging current, voltage, and protection circuits.

probabilities.description: BQ25890/BQ25895 charger IC hardware fault.
Corrosion on charging IC or surrounding passives.
Non-certified charger damaged BMIC.

repair_steps: Check for liquid damage around charging port area
test_procedure: Battery health in Settings > Battery > Battery Health

patterns.title: USB-C controller fault — USB power delivery failure
patterns.diagnosis: USB-C power delivery controller (Hydra/ACE IC on iPhone 15+) failure. Controls USB-C power negotiation and data.

probabilities.description: USB-C controller IC damaged — often from liquid or improper charger.
Physical damage to USB-C connector pins.
Power delivery negotiation firmware issue.

repair_steps: Hydra/ACE IC replacement by microsoldering specialist
test_procedure: Does device charge with Apple USB-C cable?

patterns.title: VBUS / ACOK charger detection failure
patterns.diagnosis: USB VBUS detection or ACOK (AC OK) signal failure. Device cannot properly detect charger attachment/detachment.

probabilities.description: USB controller IC damaged, cannot read VBUS.
VBUS pin on Lightning/USB-C port bent or corroded.
PMIC ACOK detection circuit failure.

test_procedure: Does device show charging animation when plugged in?

patterns.title: Battery authentication failure (no PMI) — non-genuine battery
patterns.diagnosis: iOS battery authentication challenge failed. Device detected a non-Apple-certified battery without valid PMI (Product Manufacturer ID) certificate.

probabilities.description: Replacement battery doesn't have Apple authentication chip.
Battery authentication IC failed on genuine battery.
Battery connector loosely seated, dropping auth signal.

repair_steps: Replace with Apple-certified battery (genuine or Apple Authorized provider)
Apple Service Center for official battery replacement
test_procedure: Settings > Battery > Battery Health — does it show 'Unknown Part'?

patterns.title: Undervoltage lockout / voltage fault — power rail failure
patterns.diagnosis: A power rail dropped below its minimum threshold (UVLO — Undervoltage Lockout), causing a system brown-out and panic.

probabilities.cause: Failing battery with high internal resistance
probabilities.description: Old battery with high ESR drops voltage under load.
PMIC buck converter or LDO hardware fault.
Liquid damage causing resistive path on supply rails.

test_procedure: Does device shut off suddenly at partial charge?

patterns.title: Sleep/wake power transition failure — PMIC state error
patterns.diagnosis: Device failed to complete a sleep or wake power state transition. PMIC couldn't bring power rails to required state.

probabilities.description: PMIC firmware entering invalid state during S2R/S2D.
Rail failed to reach target voltage during wake sequence.
Connected peripheral blocking wake sequence.

test_procedure: Does device panic only on sleep/wake?

patterns.title: Accelerometer sensor failure — motion sensor hardware fault
patterns.diagnosis: STMicroelectronics accelerometer (LIS2DH/LIS3DH) failed to communicate. Affects screen rotation, motion detection, AR.

probabilities.description: STMicro LIS chip failed — common after drop damage.
Solder joint failure on accelerometer I2C connection.
Flat cable carrying I2C signals to sensor damaged.

repair_steps: Screen rotation test: Settings > Accessibility > Display — auto-rotate
test_procedure: Test with a motion calibration app

patterns.title: Gyroscope failure — rotational motion sensor fault
patterns.diagnosis: 6-axis IMU gyroscope sensor (STMicro LSM6DSx) failed. Affects gaming, AR, camera stabilization, ProMotion.

probabilities.description: LSM6DS3/LSM6DSR chip failed — often post-drop.

probabilities.cause: Shared I2C bus with accelerometer fault
probabilities.description: I2C bus conflict causing gyro errors.
Gyro calibration NVRAM data corrupted.

repair_steps: Test in camera app (OIS may be affected)
test_procedure: Open Camera app — does OIS respond to motion?
Use bubble level app to test gyro

patterns.title: Magnetometer / compass failure
patterns.diagnosis: AKM magnetometer chip (AK8963/AK09918) failure. Affects Compass app, Maps navigation, and AR heading.

probabilities.description: AKM chip hardware fault — often after magnetic exposure.
Solder joint failure on magnetometer connection.
Strong magnetic fields permanently affected sensor.

repair_steps: Open Compass app — if shows error, hardware failure confirmed
test_procedure: Maps navigation heading — is it accurate?

patterns.title: LiDAR scanner failure — depth sensor hardware fault
patterns.diagnosis: LiDAR scanner hardware or driver failure. Affects AR apps, Portrait mode depth, and night camera AF.

probabilities.description: LiDAR array damaged by drop or water.
Cable connecting LiDAR to logic board damaged.
Driver crash — update resolves.

repair_steps: Test AR apps and Portrait mode depth
LiDAR module replacement (usually in camera assembly)
test_procedure: Measure app (Apple AR): can it measure distances?
Portrait mode: is depth map applied?

patterns.title: Taptic Engine / haptic motor failure
patterns.diagnosis: Taptic Engine (linear resonant actuator) hardware failure. Affects haptic feedback for keyboard, Haptic Touch, notifications.

probabilities.description: LRA coil or magnet assembly failed.
DRV2624 or equivalent driver IC fault.
Flex cable connector intermittent.

repair_steps: Haptic test: Settings > Sounds & Haptics > System Haptics toggle
Haptic driver IC replacement via microsoldering
test_procedure: Settings > Sounds & Haptics > toggle System Haptics

patterns.title: Meson touch IC failure — touchscreen controller fault
patterns.diagnosis: Meson touchscreen controller IC (Apple-designed touch IC in iPhone X+) failure. Causes ghost touches, no touch, or kernel panics.

probabilities.description: Touch IC chip failed — common after water damage.

probabilities.cause: Jumper wire failure on touch lines
probabilities.description: Thin signal trace on logic board bridging touch IC to display.
Display-to-board touch data cable damaged.

repair_steps: Jumper wire repair on logic board trace

patterns.title: Azalea touch controller failure — iPhone X touchscreen fault
patterns.diagnosis: Azalea touchscreen controller IC failure in iPhone X/XS series. Known hardware weak point requiring microsoldering.

probabilities.description: Azalea touch chip hardware fault.
Underfill under Azalea cracked from flex — common Rossmann repair.
Fine trace connecting Azalea to touch digitizer broken.

repair_steps: Known repair: Azalea IC reflowing/reballing or jumper wires
Full logic board replacement as alternative
test_procedure: Does touch fail after device gets warm?

patterns.title: TrueDepth / Face ID sensor array failure
patterns.diagnosis: TrueDepth camera system (dot projector, flood illuminator, IR camera) failure. Affects Face ID, Animoji, and depth-based portrait.

probabilities.description: VCSEL dot projector physically damaged — common after screen replacement.
Infrared camera module failed.
Notch area cable damaged during repair.

repair_steps: Test Face ID in Settings — attempt re-enrollment
Apple repair only for TrueDepth (paired to logic board)
Check for Face ID after any screen repair
test_procedure: Settings > Face ID & Passcode — can you enroll?

patterns.title: Ambient light / proximity sensor failure
patterns.diagnosis: Ambient Light Sensor (ALS) and/or proximity sensor IC failure. Causes screen not auto-dimming or staying on during calls.

probabilities.description: Combined ALS+proximity IC (STK3310 or similar) failed.
Tempered glass protector covering sensor window.
Cable from sensor assembly to logic board damaged.

repair_steps: Remove screen protector covering proximity sensor area
Test: make call, does screen turn off?
test_procedure: Auto-brightness test: Settings > Accessibility > Display > Auto-Brightness
Call test: does screen turn off during call?

patterns.title: Restore error 4013 — NAND flash storage failure during restore
patterns.diagnosis: iTunes/Finder error 4013: communication with iPhone lost during restore. Almost always indicates NAND flash hardware failure.

probabilities.description: Flash chip or ANS2 controller cannot write iOS image.
Flaky USB connection interrupted restore — try different cable/port.
Board-level short causing crash during restore.

repair_steps: Try different USB cable and computer port
If persistent: NAND hardware failure — logic board repair/replacement
test_procedure: If all fail: NAND hardware failure confirmed

patterns.title: Restore error 4014 — NAND write failure during firmware flash
patterns.diagnosis: Similar to 4013 but specifically during firmware write phase. NAND cannot be written during restore.

probabilities.description: Too many bad blocks in NAND for firmware partition.
NVMe storage controller IC failed.
Communication protocol error — try different cable.

repair_steps: Try recovery mode restore before DFU
test_procedure: Try recovery mode restore first (hold volume down + side button)

patterns.title: Restore error 9 — USB connection failure during restore
patterns.diagnosis: iTunes/Finder error 9: device disconnected or failed handshake during restore. Often Tristar/Hydra USB controller failure.

probabilities.description: USB controller IC not maintaining stable DFU connection.
Cable losing connection at DFU data rate.
Hub or port insufficient for DFU restore.

repair_steps: Use Apple cable directly into computer USB port (no hub)
test_procedure: Connect directly to computer USB-A port (not hub)

patterns.title: Restore error 14 — iOS verification or write failure
patterns.diagnosis: iTunes/Finder error 14: often NAND write error during IPSW installation, or iOS version compatibility issue.

probabilities.cause: NAND write error during iOS install
probabilities.description: Flash storage failed during iOS image write.

probabilities.cause: iOS version not signed by Apple
probabilities.description: Trying to restore to an unsigned iOS version.
IPSW file corrupted — re-download and retry.

patterns.title: Restore error 21 — power/hardware issue during restore
patterns.diagnosis: iTunes/Finder error 21: hardware issue prevented restore. Often power management or battery problem during DFU restore.

probabilities.description: Insufficient power during restore despite cable.
Power management IC caused unexpected shutdown.
Power rail dropped during restore causing crash.

repair_steps: Charge device 30+ minutes before restore attempt
Keep device plugged to power during restore

patterns.title: Restore error 4005 — DFU communication failure
patterns.diagnosis: iTunes/Finder error 4005: device in DFU mode failed to communicate properly. Usually USB or Tristar/Hydra issue.

probabilities.description: USB controller IC can't sustain DFU protocol.
Third-party cable failing at DFU data rate.
Boot ROM failing to enter valid DFU state.

repair_steps: Try directly into Mac/PC USB port
Tristar replacement if cable/port ruled out

patterns.title: Baseband firmware update failed during restore
patterns.diagnosis: iOS restore successfully wrote the main OS but failed to update baseband firmware. Device may boot but have no cellular service.

probabilities.description: Modem NOR flash cannot be written.
PMB/QCom modem chip failed.
Power dropped during sensitive baseband flash.

repair_steps: If baseband still fails: baseband chip replacement or logic board swap
test_procedure: After restore, check Settings > General > About > Modem Firmware

patterns.title: SEP firmware restore failed — Secure Enclave update error
patterns.diagnosis: DFU restore successfully wrote iOS but failed to update SEP (Secure Enclave Processor) firmware. Device may not boot or lose biometric functionality.

probabilities.description: SEP internal NVM cannot be written during restore.
Critical phase of SEP programming was interrupted.
SEP hardware failure preventing firmware write.

repair_steps: Retry DFU restore with full battery and stable USB
If persistent: SEP hardware fault — logic board replacement
test_procedure: Each time: full charge, stable cable, direct USB port

patterns.title: Restore error 4002 — device eligibility or signing failure
patterns.diagnosis: iTunes/Finder error 4002: Apple's signing server rejected the restore, or device is not eligible for the requested iOS version.

probabilities.cause: Trying to restore to unsigned iOS
probabilities.description: iOS version no longer signed by Apple.
Apple SHSH server (gs.apple.com) blocked.
Device under iCloud Activation Lock.

repair_steps: Verify iOS version is still being signed at ipsw.me

patterns.title: Prefetch abort — instruction fetch failure in kernel
patterns.diagnosis: ARM64 prefetch abort: CPU couldn't fetch an instruction from memory. Indicates corrupted code segment or bad DRAM.

probabilities.cause: DRAM bit error in code region
probabilities.description: DRAM cell fault corrupted instruction bytes.
Flash bad block in kernel partition.
Function pointer corruption jumped to invalid memory.

repair_steps: If persistent: DRAM or NAND hardware failure

patterns.title: App failed to launch — SpringBoard launch timeout
patterns.diagnosis: SpringBoard could not launch the app within the allowed time. App process either crashed immediately on launch or failed to call back.

probabilities.description: App crashes in main() before becoming responsive.
Broken app extension preventing host app launch.
App data directory corrupted.

repair_steps: Clear app data via Settings > General > iPhone Storage
test_procedure: Reinstall app fresh from App Store

patterns.title: Mach trap / IPC failure — kernel Mach messaging error
patterns.diagnosis: Mach kernel IPC or trap handling failure. Mach is the kernel's core IPC mechanism; a failure here can cause widespread system instability.

probabilities.description: Process has too many send rights — port namespace exhausted.
Receiver not draining Mach messages fast enough.
IPC space data structure corrupted.

repair_steps: Identify app with high Mach port count
test_procedure: Check for app with many open ports

patterns.title: VM compressor exhausted — all virtual memory used
patterns.diagnosis: iOS virtual memory compressor completely exhausted — no more room for even compressed pages. Emergency jetsam kills begin.

probabilities.cause: Too many large apps in background
probabilities.description: Multiple RAM-heavy apps all compressed, filling compressor.

probabilities.cause: App with memory leak filling compressor
probabilities.description: Leaking app pages filling compression store.

probabilities.cause: Large file I/O buffered in VM
probabilities.description: Large file cached in memory, not released.

patterns.title: Watchdog: syslogd log daemon hung
patterns.diagnosis: System log daemon failed watchdog. Affects system logging but may also indicate very high I/O load on storage.

probabilities.description: Log writes blocking on slow NAND.
Daemon stuck in logging loop.
No space for new log entries.

patterns.title: GPU/CPU cache coherency fault
patterns.diagnosis: Cache coherency error between CPU and GPU (or between CPU cores). Usually hardware — DRAM or SoC interconnect fault.

probabilities.description: DRAM returning stale data causing coherency violation.
Apple's AMCC/Fabric hardware bug — iOS update may fix.
Overheating causing transient coherency errors.

patterns.title: Keychain / keybag failure — secure storage error
patterns.diagnosis: iOS Keychain (backed by SEP keybag) encountered a failure. Can cause app authentication failures and system crashes if system keybag affected.

probabilities.description: Encrypted keybag data corrupted — requires device wipe.
securityd cannot communicate with SEP for key operations.
Keychain SQLite database file corrupted.

repair_steps: Re-enroll device in MDM if enterprise

patterns.title: Battery fuel gauge IC failure — battery state estimation error
patterns.diagnosis: Battery fuel gauge IC (coulomb counter) failure. Device may show incorrect battery percentage, sudden shutdowns, or fail to charge.

probabilities.description: Coulomb counter IC damaged — common after liquid damage.
Calibration data in fuel gauge NVM corrupted.
Single-wire interface between PMU and battery broken.

repair_steps: Battery replacement often resolves (gauge in battery pack)
If separate gauge IC: microsoldering replacement

patterns.title: IMS/VoLTE stack crash — voice over LTE failure
patterns.diagnosis: IMS (IP Multimedia Subsystem) stack crash affecting VoLTE calls. Causes dropped calls or cellular crashes.

probabilities.description: VoLTE/VoWiFi protocol handling edge case.
Carrier settings conflicting with iOS IMS implementation.
Carrier IMS server sending unexpected data.

repair_steps: Update carrier settings: Settings > General > About (prompts if update available)
Toggle VoLTE: Settings > Cellular > Cellular Data Options > Enable LTE

patterns.title: CPU cluster power management panic
patterns.diagnosis: Panic related to Apple Silicon CPU cluster (efficiency or performance cores) power state management. Often a firmware bug.

probabilities.description: Race condition in power state change between clusters.
Apple Matrix co-processor or CPU cache interconnect error.
Core disabled mid-execution due to thermal event.

repair_steps: Update iOS — often fixed in point releases
test_procedure: Check if specific CPU-intensive workload triggers panic

patterns.title: Uncaught NSException — Objective-C runtime exception
patterns.diagnosis: Unhandled Objective-C exception caused app to terminate. Common exceptions: array out of bounds, invalid argument, internal inconsistency.

probabilities.cause: NSRangeException — array index out of bounds
probabilities.description: Accessing array index beyond its count.

probabilities.cause: NSInvalidArgumentException — nil passed to non-nil param
probabilities.description: Objective-C method received unexpected nil argument.
Framework detected internal state violation.

test_procedure: Reproduce specific user action before crash
